races.name: Santos Tour Down Under
races.moments: Final stage: An early three-man breakaway was formed after 20 kilometres, later joined by Pavel Novák. A runaway kangaroo caused chaos in the peloton, bringing down GC leader Vine and forcing teammate Bjerg to abandon. The break was caught with one lap remaining, late attackers Buitrago, Glivar and Cepeda were neutralised in the final kilometre and Brennan won the uphill bunch sprint.

races.name: Cadel Evans Great Ocean Road Race
races.moments: Three riders escaped immediately after the start to form the early break, with Jayco and Visma controlling the peloton. The break was caught on the local circuit, where repeated attacks followed. After the final ascent of Challambra Crescent, a sizeable group came together and sprinted for victory, won by Lund Andresen.

races.name: UAE Tour
races.moments: Final Stage: A fierce fight for the break even saw attempts from Remco Evenepoel before five riders finally went clear. The break was reeled in with two kilometres remaining. Milan powered to victory in the bunch sprint, taking his third stage win of this edition. The stage was raced at the highest average speed in the history of the UAE Tour.

races.name: Omloop Nieuwsblad
races.moments: The break of the day formed after the first escape was caught. On the Molenberg, Florian Vermeersch attacked, while Rick Pluimers crashed behind him. Mathieu van der Poel skilfully avoided the fall and rode to Vermeersch with Tim van Dijke bridging across. With an acceleration on the muur Van der Poel dropped his rivals and powered to a solo victory.

races.name: Strade Bianche
races.moments: After 40 km of racing, the first meaningful breakaway formed. UAE Team Emirates controlled the peloton and stretched it on Monte Sante Marie, where everything came together and Tadej Pogačar subsequently attacked; Paul Seixas followed the longest but was dropped. Pogačar won after a 79 km solo, while Seixas dropped Isaac del Toro on the final steep kilometre to secure second.

races.name: Paris-Nice
races.moments: Final Stage: Paris-Nice stage 8 was raced aggressively, with the breakaway gradually brought back before the favourites lit up the finale. Jonas Vingegaard made the decisive move on the Côte du Linguador, with only Lenny Martinez able to follow. The pair quickly opened a gap and stayed clear into the final kilometres, while the chase fought behind for the remaining places. In the sprint, Martinez edged Vingegaard to take the stage win, while the Dane sealed the overall win. Dani Martínez held on to second in the final general classification, with Georg Steinhauser completing the podium.

races.name: Milano-Sanremo
races.moments: Milano-Sanremo began with nine riders forming the day’s breakaway. Before the Cipressa, a major crash disrupted the race, involving favorites Wout van Aert and Tadej Pogačar, forcing them to chase. Mathieu van der Poel also crashed earlier and had to recover. On the Cipressa, Pogačar attacked, with only Tom Pidcock and Van der Poel able to follow. On the Poggio, Pogačar dropped Van der Poel and rode to the finish with Pidcock, ultimately winning the sprint. Van Aert finished third after chasing back.

races.name: Ronde Van Brugge
races.moments: In a fast-paced race, repeated attacks failed to form a decisive breakaway. Strong echelons split and reduced the peloton but were eventually reeled back in. Jasper Philipsen launched his sprint slightly too early, allowing Dylan Groenewegen to come past and take the victory at the finish.

races.name: E3 Saxo Classic
races.moments: A six-rider breakaway went clear after the start. On the first passage of the Karnemelkbeekstraat a strong move formed from the peloton. On the Taaienberg, Jasper Stuyven’s acceleration stretched the peloton before Tim van Dijke attacked, with Mathieu van der Poel the only rider able to follow. Van der Poel then went clear on the Boigneberg, bridged to the leaders on the Kapelberg and dropped them for good on the Paterberg with 42 km to go. He looked set for a comfortable solo win, but the chasers came back rapidly in the closing kilometres. Van der Poel just held on, ahead of Per Strand Hagenes and Florian Vermeersch.

races.name: In Flanders Fields
races.moments: An early break of eight shaped the opening of the race, with the peloton splitting before the hill zone but regrouping before the first Kemmelberg ascent. Several attacks failed to stick until Van Aert, Van der Poel, and Vermeersch surged on the second climb of the Kemmelberg. Van Aert and Van der Poel broke clear on the final ascent with 35 km to go, and were ultimately joined by Alec Segaert with 5 km to go, but the peloton caught them in the closing kilometers, leaving Jasper Philipsen to claim the sprint victory.

races.name: Dwars door Vlaanderen
races.moments:
From the start, many attacks were launched, but none were allowed to go clear. With just over 50 kilometres to go, three riders broke away.
On the Eikenberg, Wout van Aert bridged across to the two reaming leaders. The trio stayed together until the first passage of Nokereberg, where Grégoire dropped, leaving Van Aert and Larsen out front. On the final climb, Van Aert accelerated, dropping Larsen and going solo. Behind, Filippo Ganna launched a counterattack. Ganna closed in on Van Aert in sight of the finish, launched the sprint and took victory in the fastest edition ever.

races.name: Ronde van Vlaanderen
races.moments: An early break of 13 riders built a lead of around 5:30 before being caught with 80 km remaining. Florian Vermeersch's acceleration on the Molenberg sparked the race's decisive move. Pogacar and Van der Poel dropped the field on the Oude Kwaremont, with Evenepoel unable to bridge. Pogacar attacked again with 18.5 km to go, soloing to victory in Oudenaarde ahead of Van der Poel and Evenepoel.

races.name: Paris-Roubaix
races.moments: Despite numerous attacks, no early breakaway was established. Tadej Pogačar suffered a puncture and was forced to chase, but rejoined the front group before the Trouée d’Arenberg. On that section, Mathieu van der Poel punctured twice, losing around two minutes and never returning to the first group. Pogačar and Wout van Aert attacked from a select group of seven riders and rode together to the finish, where Van Aert won the two-man sprint.
races.note: Watched on Torino streets from phone

races.name: Amstel Gold Race
races.moments: A nine-man breakaway animated the early race, with Frigo the last survivor. On the Kruisberg with 42 km to go, Evenepoel, Skjelmose and Grégoire went clear after a crash eliminated Vauquelin and Jorgenson. Grégoire was dropped on the second ascent of the Cauberg, and Evenepoel sealed the win in the sprint-à-deux.

raceTypes.name: Cadel Evans Great Ocean Road Race
